description: Master image generation prompts for TrailVolt — all remaining site image slots. Benchmarked against Stubble & Co visual quality standard. Grounded in brand-visual-foundation.md, brand-folder-analysis.md, and visual-map-final.json.
global_style_rules: Every image must be visually indistinguishable from a premium editorial gear brand shoot — Arc'teryx, Peak Design, Aer quality
The module attachment rail on the front face must be visible in ALL studio product shots — it is TrailVolt's primary visual differentiator
Fabric texture must be legible: 600D crosshatch ballistic nylon, 500D Cordura — not smooth, not shiny, not plastic-looking
YKK zipper pulls appear as matte metal — precise, weighty, never plastic injection-moulded
Studio backgrounds are always dark navy (#0B1626) — never black, charcoal, grey, or white
Electric mint (#3FFFAB) appears in exactly one role per image — a directional edge glow or system diagram connection lines only
People in lifestyle shots are ALWAYS in motion or at work — never posed, never smiling at camera, never looking directly at lens
No text, watermarks, brand logos, or graphic overlays on any image — not on pack, not on background, nowhere
Locations must be SPECIFIC: Dublin Georgian streets, Wicklow Mountains boggy trails, Dublin Airport, Barcelona old town, Amsterdam canals — never generic
No AI artefact signatures: no melting zippers, no distorted hardware, no impossible reflections, no phantom stitching
Lifestyle shots must feel like they were taken on an actual day by a journalist who happened to be there
reference_quality_standard: Stubble & Co product precision (consistent angles, studio discipline, material naming) + Arc'teryx lifestyle emotion (earned environments, real terrain, motion) + Monocle editorial authenticity (candid, purposeful, non-performative). Images must pass the Gear Patrol test: would this look at home in their annual gear guide without a caption?

prompts.purpose: Lifestyle hero for the Trail 28L line card. Must make an outdoor-oriented buyer feel 'that is my Saturday pack'.
prompts.emotional_role: The call of the outdoors — Irish terrain, earned light, real trail
prompts.commercial_role: Converts outdoor-lifestyle segment to Trail 28L — the highest-margin product
prompts.alt_text: Hiker on Irish mountain trail with TrailVolt Trail 28L Forest Green — Wild Atlantic Way terrain
prompts.short_prompt: Hiker from behind on a misty Irish highland trail, Forest Green trail pack loaded with visible bottle holder, cinematic golden-hour light, no text anywhere.
prompts.detailed_prompt: A cinematic outdoor lifestyle photograph taken in the Wicklow Mountains or Wild Atlantic Way: rounded green ridgelines, overcast sky with a narrow band of warm golden light breaking at the distant horizon, wisps of mist in the valley below, rough boggy trail surface with visible heather and dark peat. A single hiker — gender-neutral to male, early 30s, lean and capable — is mid-stride moving away from the camera along the trail. They are not resting, not looking back, not posed. The TrailVolt Trail 28L backpack in Forest Green (#2D5016) is on their back. The green reads unmistakably as Forest Green — deep, natural, not olive-drab, not khaki, not black. The pack is visibly loaded: side compression straps drawn taut against the packed contents; the load-distribution harness is engaged across the hips. On the left side panel of the pack, the Bottle Holder Module holds a matte stainless steel water bottle — the top of the bottle extends just above the holder rim. The Cordura 500D shell has a faint sheen of moisture from the Irish weather — DWR-treated surface with micro water droplets visible under side light. The camera captures this as if from a tracking position beside or slightly behind and above the hiker — a filmmaker's observational shot, not a posed portrait. Focal length equivalent: 70mm. The landscape occupies the upper 50% of the frame. The pack and hiker fill the lower-centre. Golden-hour rim light from behind the hiker creates a faint silhouette-edge separation. The mood: earned, elemental, real. No studio. No flash. No text. No brand marks. No logos anywhere in the image — not on the pack, not on clothing, not on the environment.
prompts.negative_prompt: text on bag, brand name on fabric, printed letters, embossed text, logo on pack, typography on backpack, writing on fabric, labelling on product, text overlay anywhere in image, posed summit shot, looking at camera, smiling, alpine peaks, Swiss Alps, Austrian mountains, tropical terrain, studio environment, white background, bright sunny blue sky, stock photo hiker, generic hiking catalogue composition, green appearing as olive or black, missing bottle holder module, overcrowded frame, too-small pack in composition, orange or amber text on any surface
prompts.style_notes: Reference: Arc'teryx campaign photography. Fjällräven editorial. Patagonia lifestyle imagery. The Irish terrain specificity is critical — this is not the Alps. It is the Wild Atlantic Way: green, moody, maritime, boggy.
prompts.composition_notes: Hiker occupies centre-lower third of frame. Pack fills approximately 30-40% of the total frame. Horizon line in upper quarter creates landscape dominance while keeping pack as primary subject.
prompts.lighting_notes: Golden-hour backlighting from low on the horizon. Side light from the break in clouds. Natural overcast ambient fill from the sky. No artificial lighting.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: Tribe member Aoife Murphy hiked the Wild Atlantic Way for six days with her Trail 28L — 'I didn't miss a single thing I left behind.' This is her story. This image is for everyone who looks at a trail map on a Thursday and starts mentally packing.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co wins through precision. TrailVolt must win through precision AND emotional terrain specificity. The Irish landscape is the brand's unique lifestyle territory — no competitor owns it.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The logo's inverted triangle pointing down is directional — descending, moving, trail-going. The hand-drawn quality demands authenticity. This image must feel like it was not set up.

prompts.purpose: Lifestyle hero for the Travel 30L line card. Must make a frequent flyer feel 'no checked bag, no drama, no limit'.
prompts.emotional_role: Liberation — one carry-on, every destination, no compromise
prompts.commercial_role: Converts the frequent flyer and digital nomad segment to Travel 30L
prompts.alt_text: Traveller at airport departure gate with TrailVolt Travel 30L Slate Grey — carry-on ready
prompts.short_prompt: Confident traveller mid-stride through modern airport terminal with Slate Grey travel pack, departure gates in background, natural ambient terminal light.
prompts.detailed_prompt: A sharp editorial-style lifestyle photograph taken inside a modern international airport — the visual language is a European hub: Dublin, Lisbon, or Barcelona airport. Wide, high-ceilinged terminal with natural light flooding through large glass panels, departure gates visible in the mid-distance with flight information screens. The floors are polished concrete or light stone. A single traveller — male or female, late 20s to mid-30s, well-dressed and purposeful — is mid-stride through the terminal walking toward a departure gate. They are not rushing but they are clearly moving with intent. They are not looking at the camera. The TrailVolt Travel 30L in Slate Grey (#6B7280) is on their back. The pack is the IATA cabin-compliant size: it looks right for an overhead bin, not a hiking trip. The clamshell structure of the Travel pack is subtly visible — the wrap-around zip line shows the pack's full opening capability. The Slate Grey colourway reads clearly as a lighter, more professional shade than black — the business traveller's colour. The person is wearing smart casual: dark chinos or slim trousers, a clean shirt or light jacket. No branded clothing visible. The airport environment frames the journey without dominating the pack. Focal length equivalent: 50mm. The image has genuine depth — foreground, mid-ground (traveller and pack), background (terminal architecture). Natural terminal lighting — warm from above, cooler from the glass panels. The overall feeling: autonomy, efficiency, the confidence of someone who travels constantly and has figured out exactly what they need.
prompts.negative_prompt: text on bag, logo on pack, writing on clothing, posed composition, camera-facing, smiling at lens, generic stock photo travel cliché, waving at departure gate, pulling wheeled luggage alongside (no suitcases), shuttle bus, taxi rank, hotel lobby, beach resort, costume or formal attire, budget airline interior, overhead bin (this is outside the gate), overcrowded frame, empty terminal that looks abandoned
prompts.style_notes: Reference: Away luggage campaign photography. The Monocle guide to travel imagery. The balance is: the terminal is recognisable but not dominant, the person is purposeful but not performative, the pack is Slate Grey and clearly designed for exactly this moment.
prompts.composition_notes: Person fills roughly 40% of frame at 2/3 horizontal position. Terminal architecture in background provides spatial depth. Pack on back is clearly Slate Grey and structured.
prompts.lighting_notes: Natural terminal ambient — warm overhead panels, cool natural daylight from glass facades. No flash. The Slate Grey pack must catch enough light to differentiate from Core Black.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: Marco Lazzarini — Digital Nomad, Barcelona — has taken the Travel 30L to 14 countries without ever checking a bag. This image is his story. And every person who has ever paid a gate check fee they didn't need to pay.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co describes product use in copy ('work to weekend'). TrailVolt must show it — specifically. The airport terminal is the most specific and commercially potent environment for the Travel line.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: Tagline 'ONE PACK. EVERY HORIZON.' — the airport is one of TrailVolt's clearest horizons. The Slate Grey colourway was described as 'office-appropriate' — but this shot shows it is also terminal-appropriate.

prompts.purpose: Material proof shot — communicates construction quality through close-up. The tactile image for buyers who need to see what they're buying before they trust the price.
prompts.emotional_role: Confidence through evidence — this is what premium gear looks like at 5cm distance
prompts.commercial_role: Overcomes the 'but is it actually quality?' objection before it's raised
prompts.alt_text: TrailVolt Core Pack detail — 600D ballistic nylon weave texture, YKK zipper pull, module attachment rail
prompts.short_prompt: Extreme close-up of a black modular backpack's front face — visible 600D nylon crosshatch weave, matte YKK zipper pull, and module attachment rail connection slots in crisp detail.
prompts.detailed_prompt: An extreme close-up product photograph of the front face panel of a Core Black TrailVolt backpack — shot with a macro or medium telephoto lens at approximately 30-40cm working distance. The image is a study in material texture and hardware precision. In the centre of the frame: the horizontal module attachment rail — a row of matte stainless steel connection slots approximately 15mm wide each, separated by narrow matte nylon bridges. The slots are recessed into the pack face, with clean machined edges. The surrounding fabric: 600D ballistic nylon in Core Black, with the crosshatch weave clearly visible — approximately 2mm spacing in the weave pattern, slightly different light reflection between the warp and weft threads. The fabric surface is tightly woven, dense, slightly tactile-looking. Crossing into the lower portion of the frame: a YKK zipper pull — matte black metal, a small rectangular loop on a slider. The slider itself shows the YKK branding if clearly detailed, but the priority is the hardware quality: it looks weighty, precise, not plastic. Lighting: single raking key light from the left at a very low angle (approximately 15-20 degrees from the surface) to reveal maximum texture in the nylon weave. A soft fill from the right prevents shadow crushing. Dark navy background around the edges of the frame fades to near-black. Extreme depth of field — everything within the 20cm frame is sharp. No people, no hands, no props. The entire image is a single material moment — the proof that this is a premium product.
prompts.negative_prompt: smooth or plastic-looking fabric, shiny nylon, cheap-looking hardware, plastic zipper, entire bag in frame, lifestyle context, hands, people, white background, blurry fabric texture, out-of-focus hardware, generic backpack details not matching the described features
prompts.style_notes: Reference: Peak Design product detail photography. The hardware photography standard of premium watch brands — every surface detail earns its presence in the frame.
prompts.composition_notes: Tightly cropped — fabric texture and rail dominate the frame. No horizon, no background, no context. Pure material study.
prompts.lighting_notes: Low-angle raking light at 15 degrees from one side to reveal fabric weave texture. This is the definitive lighting technique for showing textile quality.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: The brand's competitive positioning includes: 'Materials disclosed per product: denier count, weave type, coating spec, hardware supplier.' This image is the visual proof of that commitment. The feature showcase section on index.html exists specifically for this moment.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co communicates material quality through copy (naming Millerain Tekwax®). TrailVolt can go further by also showing it photographically. This is the upgrade opportunity.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The logo's hand-drawn quality says 'craft over polish'. This image must show that craft exists at the material level — in the weave, in the hardware, in the connection system.

prompts.purpose: Visual proof of the lifetime repair programme. Communicates: this brand built its product to be fixed, not replaced.
prompts.emotional_role: Responsibility and permanence — the opposite of fast fashion
prompts.commercial_role: Justifies premium price through visible longevity commitment. Converts sustainability-motivated buyers.
prompts.alt_text: TrailVolt spare parts laid out — YKK zipper pull, Duraflex buckle, module connector, nylon thread — lifetime repair programme
prompts.short_prompt: Flat-lay of premium backpack hardware spare parts on dark navy surface — YKK zipper pull, Duraflex buckle, thread spool, module connector, arranged with precision and a whisper of mint light.
prompts.detailed_prompt: A meticulously arranged flat-lay photograph on a dark navy (#0B1626) surface — the TrailVolt lifetime repair programme communicated in a single frame. The items, each photographed with the precision of a product shot: (1) a YKK zipper pull — matte black metal, oval loop profile, slider precisely placed, (2) a Duraflex buckle — dark matte plastic, the geometric clip mechanism visible, showing its load-rated construction, (3) a small spool of dark navy thread — fabric thread for stitching repair, (4) a module attachment connector — a small stainless steel and nylon piece, approximately 3cm, showing the connection mechanism that links module to pack, (5) a small rectangle of 600D ballistic nylon fabric swatch — showing the material that can be used for panel repair, (6) a YKK zipper slider (different from the pull) — the main body of a zip mechanism. The items are arranged in a deliberate but not overly designed layout — each item has breathing room, each is identifiably itself. The arrangement suggests: these are real parts, organised by a person who cares about the system. A very subtle electric mint (#3FFFAB) accent light grazes across the module connector and the YKK zipper pull from one side — as if these pieces are being illuminated by the product they belong to. The light is a whisper, not a flood. Studio macro photography quality — each piece is photographed at high resolution. Extreme sharpness across all items. The dark navy background is seamless, vignetting very slightly at corners. No text, no labels, no stickers visible.
prompts.negative_prompt: white background, grey background, hands, people, sewing machine, factory setting, generic craft supplies, bright coloured thread, cheap plastic hardware, neon accent light, cluttered arrangement, items touching each other, unlabelled generic hardware that doesn't match the described parts, text or pricing labels on any item
prompts.style_notes: Reference: Apple spare parts programme photography. The Patagonia Worn Wear repair programme visual language. The goal is to make these parts feel as precise and considered as the product they service.
prompts.composition_notes: Six items arranged in a loose grid or natural scatter with deliberate spacing. Dark navy surface occupies 80% of the frame — the items are islands in the brand's universe.
prompts.lighting_notes: Primary light from upper-right at 45 degrees, creating sharp definition on each piece. Mint accent from lower-left, very low power, grazing the metal components only.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: The sustainability.html page section on the repair programme is currently text-only. This image makes the promise concrete: here are the actual parts. This is what a lifetime commitment looks like.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co doesn't have a visible repair programme. This is TrailVolt's competitive advantage — and this image is the visual proof of it.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The hand-drawn logo symbol says this brand was built by a person, not a factory. Spare parts laid out with care say the same thing — this is a craftsperson's approach to longevity.

prompts.purpose: Bundle section image — the complete system shot for the shop upsell section. Shows pack + key modules together.
prompts.emotional_role: Completion — the full picture of what TrailVolt actually is as a system
prompts.commercial_role: Drives bundle purchases and increases average order value — the highest AOV conversion on shop page
prompts.alt_text: TrailVolt Core Pack with selected modules — the complete modular system
prompts.short_prompt: Studio flat-lay of TrailVolt Core Pack in Core Black with 4 key modules arranged around it on dark navy, cinematic rim lighting, electric mint connection thread.
prompts.detailed_prompt: A precise studio product photograph — a curated bundle composition. The TrailVolt Core Pack in Core Black is the central element, photographed front-facing at a slight 3/4 angle showing the module attachment rail prominently. The pack is elevated slightly to cast a clean shadow on the dark navy (#0B1626) seamless background. Arranged around the pack, positioned to suggest connection rather than random placement: (1) the Tech Organizer Module — a flat multi-pocket pouch, positioned to the upper-right of the pack, (2) the Power Bank Module — a brick-shaped unit, positioned to the lower-right, (3) the Bottle Holder Module — a cylindrical sleeve, positioned to the left, (4) a partial view of the Chest Phone Module positioned top-left. Each module is photographed product-quality: sharp, correct matte finish, YKK hardware visible. Very subtle electric mint (#3FFFAB) light traces connect from the pack's attachment rail outward toward each module — not graphic arrows, but hair-thin photographic light threads suggesting magnetic connection. The lighting: cinematic three-point with a very soft rim light creating silhouette separation from the background. The mint accent light comes from behind and below the central pack, barely visible at the rail. No text, no price tags, no labels. Pure product composition.
prompts.negative_prompt: white background, text labels, price tags, all 8 modules (too cluttered), generic accessories, unrecognisable module shapes, graphic arrows or digital overlays, neon saturation, flat unlit composition, blurry modules, modules that don't match described designs
prompts.style_notes: Reference: Apple ecosystem product shots (iPhone + AirPods + Apple Watch in one frame). The bundle image must communicate 'system' — not a random pile of accessories, but a coherent set of solutions.
prompts.composition_notes: Central pack at 45% of frame width. Four modules positioned at cardinal points with asymmetric spacing to feel curated, not mechanical. Each module has breathing room.
prompts.lighting_notes: Three-point studio lighting with cinematic precision. Electric mint from behind pack at floor level, barely catching the rail. Rim light from upper-left creates pack definition.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: The shop.html bundle section is the highest-AOV conversion point on the page. This image must make the buyer think 'I want the pack AND those modules' in a single glance.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co doesn't do bundle photography — they show products individually. This is TrailVolt's advantage: the modularity IS the product story, and bundle photography proves it visually.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The logo's systematic geometry (the precise triangle) suggests a brand that thinks in systems. The bundle image must convey that same systematic thinking applied to product.

prompts.section: Trail Line — Forest Green product card
prompts.purpose: Trail 28L Forest Green product card — the Trail line's primary colourway and bestseller.
prompts.emotional_role: Outdoor ownership — this pack belongs on a real trail
prompts.commercial_role: Primary conversion for the Trail line — green is the outdoor buyer's instinct
prompts.alt_text: TrailVolt Trail 28L Forest Green modular backpack — 500D Cordura shell, module attachment rail visible
prompts.short_prompt: Studio product photography of a 28L Forest Green trail backpack, 500D Cordura texture, visible module rail, dark navy gradient background, three-point cinematic lighting.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Clean studio product photography of the TrailVolt Trail 28L in Forest Green (#2D5016). The pack is photographed at a strong 3/4 angle showing both the front face and one side panel — the same angle as pack-everyday-black.webp for collection visual consistency. Background: dark navy (#0B1626) seamless paper. The pack form: 28L — slightly larger than the Everyday, with a taller top and more pronounced shoulder harness. The module attachment rail is visible across the front face as a horizontal row of matte stainless connection slots. The Forest Green colourway: deep, natural green — not olive, not khaki, not military. It reads as the colour of mature Irish hedgerow or Scottish Highland grass in overcast light. The shell material is 500D Cordura: slightly rougher than the 600D nylon of the Everyday, with a visible but more compact weave pattern. The load-distribution harness is visible at the top and sides — suggesting this pack is built for carrying actual weight. The top handle is reinforced-looking. YKK zipper pulls matte black throughout. No hydration hose ports (the hose sleeve is internal and not visible from outside). Lighting: same three-point setup as the Everyday product cards — key from upper-right, soft fill from left, very thin rim from behind. No electric mint in shop cards. Pure product commercial photography. No people, no hands, no text.
prompts.negative_prompt: white or grey background, smooth or plastic-looking Cordura, olive or khaki green instead of forest green, general hiking bag without module rail, hydration hose port visible externally, too-bright or saturated green colour, text on bag, people, hands, mint glow (not in this shot), slouching form, uneven lighting
prompts.style_notes: Same photographic standard as pack-everyday-black.webp — the collection must look consistent. Only the colourway and pack form change between cards.
prompts.composition_notes: 3/4 angle, pack filling 85% of frame width. Same camera height and angle as other pack cards for collection consistency.
prompts.lighting_notes: Three-point commercial product lighting — key upper-right, fill left, rim from behind. No accent colour. Forest Green must be correctly rendered — test that the colour is not underexposed to black.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: Forest Green is described in brand-visual-foundation.md as 'a limited outdoor-inspired colourway; Trail favourite'. This is the card that outdoor buyers look for.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co shows each colourway as a separate product card with identical angle and lighting. TrailVolt must do the same — nine pack cards, same angle, different colourway.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: Forest Green in the brand system is the trail environment's colour — it belongs in its natural context even in studio, because the colourway itself tells the story.

prompts.section: Travel Line — Core Black product card
prompts.purpose: Travel 30L Core Black product card — the largest pack in the line-up, designed for 4-night trips.
prompts.emotional_role: Clean confidence — the traveller who never checks a bag
prompts.commercial_role: Primary conversion for the Travel line
prompts.alt_text: TrailVolt Travel 30L Core Black modular backpack — IATA cabin-compliant, clamshell opening
prompts.short_prompt: Studio product photography of a 30L matte black IATA travel backpack, clamshell side visible, module rail, 420D ripstop nylon texture, dark navy background.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Clean studio product photography of the TrailVolt Travel 30L in Core Black. 3/4 angle showing front and side panel — same angle standard as all pack product cards. The Travel 30L is the largest of the three lines: visibly taller and wider than the Everyday 22L, but still structured and clearly designed for carry-on travel rather than expedition. The key visual feature of the Travel line: the clamshell opening — the side profile shows a continuous zip that wraps from the bottom, up the side, and across the top. This detail communicates the pack's flat-open capability for airport security and packing. The module attachment rail on the front face is visible: same matte stainless connection system as all other packs. The shell material: 420D Ripstop Nylon with DWR C6 coating — slightly lighter visual texture than the 600D of the Everyday, with the ripstop diamond pattern faintly visible in the surface. Matte Core Black colourway. Lockable zip pulls: slightly different profile from standard YKK pulls — these are lockable, showing a small loop for a security lock. Background: dark navy seamless. Three-point lighting, no mint accent. No people, no hands, no text.
prompts.negative_prompt: white or grey background, hydration pack silhouette, hiking frame visible through back, missing clamshell zip detail, module rail absent, generic black backpack without travel features, people, hands, text, logos, slouching form
prompts.style_notes: The Travel pack must read as 'business travel meets weekend adventure' — not a hiking bag, not a briefcase. The clamshell zip is the visual signature of this line.
prompts.composition_notes: 3/4 angle identical to other pack cards. The side panel showing the clamshell wrap-around zip is important — position the 3/4 angle to make it visible.
prompts.lighting_notes: Three-point commercial lighting. The ripstop texture should be subtly visible — not the dominant feature, but confirmable on close inspection.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: The Travel 30L is described as 'IATA cabin-compliant, clamshell flat-open, detachable 8L day bag, lockable zips'. Each of these features should be subtly implied in the product shot.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co shows their Roll Top at multiple angles. TrailVolt's clamshell is the differentiating feature — make it visible in the 3/4 angle.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: Core Black is the 'essential, versatile default' — in the Travel line it reads as the business traveller's choice.

prompts.section: Everyday Line — Slate Grey product card
prompts.purpose: Everyday 22L Slate Grey product card — the office-appropriate colourway.
prompts.emotional_role: Professional restraint — understated but unmistakably premium
prompts.commercial_role: Secondary Everyday conversion for buyers who need professional appearance
prompts.alt_text: TrailVolt Everyday 22L Slate Grey modular backpack — professional colourway
prompts.short_prompt: Studio product photography of a 22L Slate Grey modular backpack, module rail visible, 600D nylon texture, same angle as Core Black card, dark navy gradient background.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Clean studio product photography of the TrailVolt Everyday 22L in Slate Grey (#6B7280). Exact same camera angle, distance, and framing as pack-everyday-black.webp and pack-trail-forest.webp — the collection must look consistent. The Slate Grey colourway: a medium cool grey, lighter than the navy background, clearly distinct from Core Black. Not silver, not charcoal — a deliberate grey-blue tone that reads as professional and understated. The shell is the same 600D ballistic nylon as the Core Black version — the crosshatch weave is visible but appears in a lighter, slightly less textured way due to the grey colourway (grey fabric absorbs less contrast than black). The module attachment rail on the front face is the same matte stainless design — it pops more against the lighter grey fabric than it does on black. YKK zipper pulls: matte gunmetal grey, matching the colourway. Three-point studio lighting — same setup as all pack cards. No electric mint. No people. No text.
prompts.negative_prompt: silver or bright metallic grey, charcoal (too dark), navy grey (too blue), white or pale grey, wrong colourway that could be confused with black, module rail missing, people, hands, text, logos, generic backpack silhouette without modular features
prompts.style_notes: The Slate Grey must look like a considered, premium choice — not a less-interesting version of black. It has its own identity: office, professional, versatile.
prompts.composition_notes: Identical framing to pack-everyday-black.webp. The only difference is the colourway.
prompts.lighting_notes: Same three-point setup. Ensure Slate Grey does not underexpose or overexpose — it should render as a distinct mid-tone grey.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: Brand-visual-foundation.md describes Slate Grey as 'understated, office-ready, most popular for Travel'. This card must communicate that understated quality.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Consistency across colourway cards is essential. Stubble & Co achieves this — TrailVolt must too.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The logo uses a two-tone navy — navy-to-grey-blue. Slate Grey is the packward expression of that second tone.

prompts.purpose: Primary image for the product configurator — shown by default when the page loads with Everyday 22L selected.
prompts.emotional_role: This is what I'm building — photorealistic product visualisation at point of purchase
prompts.commercial_role: The image the buyer stares at while choosing modules. Must be immaculate — any quality issue here directly reduces conversion.
prompts.alt_text: TrailVolt Everyday 22L Core Black — product configurator view
prompts.short_prompt: Square-format studio product shot of the TrailVolt Everyday 22L Core Black, front-facing with slight 3/4 tilt, module rail clearly visible, all features legible, dark navy background with subtle mint edge glow.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Square-format ultra-premium studio product photography of the TrailVolt Everyday 22L Core Pack in Core Black. This is a more frontal angle than the shop cards — the pack is almost face-on but with a 10-15 degree tilt to the left to create depth without losing front-face detail. This framing is specifically to maximise the visibility of the module attachment rail system across the front face — the buyer is actively considering which modules to add, so they need to see exactly where modules attach. The module attachment rail: a horizontal row of 6 rectangular stainless steel connection slots across the middle of the front face. Each slot approximately 15mm wide, with visible depth and edge machining. This is the most important detail in this image. Above the rail: the rapid-access front organiser zip — a horizontal zip across the upper front face with the same matte YKK hardware. The main compartment wrap-around zip runs the perimeter. The shoulder straps emerge from the top, partially visible at the frame edges. The shell: 600D ballistic nylon crosshatch, visible weave pattern. Background: dark navy (#0B1626) seamless. An extremely subtle electric mint glow originates from directly behind and below the pack, catching only the very bottom edge and the module rail slots — this is the 'technology is alive in this product' signal. Focal length: 85mm equivalent. Ultra-sharp across the entire front face. The buyer can zoom into this image and see every detail clearly. No hands, no people, no text, no props.
prompts.negative_prompt: lifestyle context, hands in frame, full side angle without front face visible, missing module rail, smooth plastic-looking fabric, bright chrome or silver hardware, generic backpack silhouette, very high contrast mint glow (whisper only), text anywhere, blurry front panel details
prompts.style_notes: This is the product configurator hero image — it must perform at multiple zoom levels. At thumbnail: the pack is clearly Core Black and premium. At 2× zoom: every material detail, hardware piece, and module connection point is sharp.
prompts.composition_notes: Square format, pack centred with slight 3/4 tilt. Pack fills 90% of frame height. Module rail positioned at visual centre of frame — it is the focal point.
prompts.lighting_notes: Five-point studio setup for maximum detail: key from upper-left, fill from right, rim from upper-right for pack silhouette, base light from below for undercarriage detail, mint glow from directly behind at floor level.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: The product.html configurator is where the conversion happens. This image is seen by every buyer who reaches the decision point. Quality here is non-negotiable.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co's product cards are clean and consistent. The configurator image must exceed that standard — because it is seen at the moment of maximum buyer engagement.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: This image is the closest TrailVolt gets to a 'showcase' shot — the logo's precision and the product's precision should feel unified here.

prompts.purpose: Product card for the Chest Phone Module — magnetic chest-mount, instant access, €29.
prompts.emotional_role: Immediate access — your phone is always exactly where you need it without stopping or removing the pack
prompts.commercial_role: Add-to-cart for €29 — the most impulse-purchaseable module
prompts.alt_text: Chest Phone Module — magnetic chest mount for TrailVolt Core Pack, instant phone access
prompts.short_prompt: Studio product photography of a slim black chest-mount phone pouch module on dark navy, magnetic clasp visible, designed to snap onto a backpack's chest strap.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Clean studio product photography of the TrailVolt Chest Phone Module. The module is a slim rectangular pouch designed to mount on a pack's chest strap or front attachment rail. It is matte black — same 600D nylon as the main pack. The front face shows: a magnetic snap closure running along the top edge (the flap folds down to reveal a phone-sized interior). The dimensions suggest it holds a phone up to 6.7 inches with a slim case. On the back: the attachment mechanism — the connection hardware that clips to the pack's chest strap or magnetic module rail. This hardware is precisely visible: stainless steel clip with a matte finish. The module is shown slightly angled — facing front with a 20-degree tilt to show the attachment mechanism on the back panel edge. Background: dark navy (#0B1626) seamless. Three-point studio lighting — key from upper-left, fill from right, thin rim from behind. Very subtle electric mint reflection on the attachment clip hardware. Ultra-sharp. No people, no hands, no text. The module looks like a small premium product in its own right — not a bag accessory.
prompts.negative_prompt: white background, bright coloured module, cheap plastic clip, generic phone armband, module too large or too small, module that doesn't suggest chest/strap mounting, hands, text, logos, watermark
prompts.style_notes: Each module must feel like a premium accessory in its own right — not a cheap add-on. The photography standard should be: if this appeared on a premium accessory brand's site as a standalone product, would it look at home? Yes.
prompts.composition_notes: Module fills 70% of frame. Slight 20-degree tilt to show both face and attachment clip edge. Dark navy breathing room around the product.
prompts.lighting_notes: Three-point lighting, mint only on attachment hardware. Module face should be fully detailed.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: The first module listed. Sets the visual standard for all 8 module cards — they must be consistent.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co's module-style accessories are shown individually on clean backgrounds. Same principle here — isolation creates focus and premium perception.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The module cards collectively tell the story of the system. Each one is a chapter. The chest phone module is Chapter 1: access.

prompts.purpose: Product card for the Tech Organizer Module — cables, adapters, SD cards, pen loop. €35.
prompts.emotional_role: Order from chaos — every cable in its designated place, always findable
prompts.commercial_role: Add-to-cart for €35 — the most 'obvious' module for digital professionals
prompts.alt_text: Tech Organizer Module — cables, adapters, SD card slots, pen loop organiser for TrailVolt
prompts.short_prompt: Studio product photography of an open tech organizer module showing internal pockets, cable loops, SD card slots, and pen loop — on dark navy background, matte black finish.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Clean studio product photography of the TrailVolt Tech Organizer Module — shown slightly open or partially unfolded to reveal its internal organisation system. The module: a flat bifold-style pouch in matte black 600D nylon. The visible interior features: a row of 4 elastic cable loops (for USB-C, Lightning, and other cables), 4 SD card slots, a small mesh pocket, and a pen loop. The exterior: smooth with a YKK zip across the top. The back face shows the attachment hardware — same stainless clip system as all modules. The module is shown at a 30-degree tilt: one half showing the open interior, one half showing the clean exterior face. Background: dark navy. Three-point lighting. A subtle electric mint catch-light on the internal SD card slots — as if the technology of what these slots hold is alive. No people, no hands, no text. Sharp enough to count the individual cable loops.
prompts.negative_prompt: white background, module closed with no interior visible, cables or devices already inserted (clutter), bright coloured interior, cheap-looking elastic, plastic zippers, hands, text, logos
prompts.style_notes: The partially-open view communicates function without requiring copy. The buyer understands what this does in one frame.
prompts.composition_notes: Module at 30-degree open position, filling 75% of frame. Interior organisation clearly visible in the open half.
prompts.lighting_notes: Three-point, mint accent on internal SD slots only.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: The tech organiser is for digital professionals — the same buyers who read Gear Patrol. This card must match that editorial standard.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Interior organisation must be shown — it's what differentiates this module from a generic case.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The system logic of the logo (triangle + wordmark) is mirrored in the organisation logic of this module. Everything has its place.

prompts.purpose: Product card for the Power Bank Module — 10,000mAh, USB-A + USB-C. €39 — highest-priced module.
prompts.emotional_role: Never stranded — always charged regardless of location or duration
prompts.commercial_role: Add-to-cart for €39 — positioned as the premium tech module
prompts.alt_text: Power Bank Module — 10,000mAh integrated power bank with USB-A and USB-C ports for TrailVolt
prompts.short_prompt: Studio photography of a compact black power bank module with USB-A and USB-C ports visible, stainless attachment clip, on dark navy, electric mint LED indicator glow.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Clean studio product photography of the TrailVolt Power Bank Module. The module: a compact rectangular brick in matte black — approximately the size of a deck of cards, slightly thicker. On the front face: a USB-A port and a USB-C port, both visible and precisely detailed — the metallic connector geometry is sharp and weighty. A small LED indicator strip (2-3 LEDs) showing charge level. On the side: the stainless steel attachment clip for the module rail connection. The build quality visible in the photography: the USB port surrounds are machined aluminium or matte black ABS with no visible seam lines. The module is shown at a 15-degree tilt to show both the port face and the attachment clip side. Background: dark navy. On the LED strip: a very subtle electric mint (#3FFFAB) glow — as if the power bank is at full charge. This mint LED catch-light is the most explicit use of the brand colour in the module series. Three-point studio lighting. No people, no hands, no text. No cables inserted.
prompts.negative_prompt: white or grey background, no ports visible, bright LED glow (whisper only), cables plugged in, hands, text anywhere, logos visible, cheap plastic housing, round or irregular form (it's a rectangular brick), missing attachment clip
prompts.style_notes: The power bank is the most 'tech' object in the module series — it must look like it belongs in a premium tech accessory lineup. The LED mint glow is the most brand-aligned detail in this module.
prompts.composition_notes: Module at 15-degree tilt showing USB ports on face and attachment clip on side edge. Ports occupy upper portion of visible face.
prompts.lighting_notes: Three-point lighting. Mint accent from LED strip — the only module where mint appears from the product itself, not from an external light source.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: The Power Bank Module was community-voted (Nadia Voronova's archetype is tied to this module). It is the most expensive module — the image must justify €39.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Premium tech products are photographed with extreme port detail visible. This image must meet that standard.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The 'Volt' in TrailVolt refers to electrical energy. The Power Bank Module is the most literal expression of the brand name — the image should feel electric.

prompts.purpose: Product card for the Bottle Holder Module — side-swing, retractable. €18.
prompts.emotional_role: Hydration always accessible — the detail that makes this pack trail-ready
prompts.commercial_role: Add-to-cart for €18 — the most functional and trail-oriented module
prompts.alt_text: Bottle Holder Module — side-swing retractable holder for TrailVolt, shown with stainless water bottle
prompts.short_prompt: Studio photography of a side-swing bottle holder module with a matte stainless water bottle seated in the holder, retractable arm extended, dark navy background.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Clean studio product photography of the TrailVolt Bottle Holder Module. This module is unique in that it has a moving part: the retractable side-swing arm that folds flat when empty and swings out to hold a bottle. The module is shown in the extended position with an 750ml matte stainless steel water bottle seated in the nylon sleeve — the bottle top extends approximately 3cm above the holder rim. The arm mechanism is visible: a nylon-webbing and stainless steel bracket that swings from the attachment point on the pack to hold the bottle away from the body. The sleeve itself: a cylindrical sleeve in matte black nylon with an adjustable bungee cord at the bottom for bottle security. Background: dark navy. The stainless water bottle: no branding visible on the bottle itself. The combination of the matte black nylon module and the matte silver steel bottle creates a premium two-tone detail composition. Three-point lighting. A very subtle electric mint glow catches the stainless steel attachment arm mechanism. No people, no hands, no text.
prompts.negative_prompt: white or grey background, module in retracted position (arm should be extended and bottle shown), branded water bottle, plastic water bottle, hands, text, logos, generic bottle holder without visible mechanism, module collapsed flat
prompts.style_notes: The bottle holder is the module most prominently featured in lifestyle shots (visible in line-card-trail.webp). The product card must match that context — this is trail gear, made precise.
prompts.composition_notes: Module with extended arm and bottle filling 80% of frame height. The arm mechanism is partially visible to show the engineering.
prompts.lighting_notes: Three-point. Mint on the stainless steel arm mechanism only.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: The bottle holder is visible in the trail lifestyle images — this is the module that makes the Trail pack unmistakably trail-ready.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: The bottle holder is a functional detail, not a decorative one. The photography must communicate function — show it working (bottle in holder, arm extended).
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The outdoor functionality of this module connects directly to the 'Trail' brand environment — Wicklow, Wild Atlantic Way, real terrain.

prompts.section: Tribe Cards — Aoife Murphy, Adventure Hiker, Donegal
prompts.purpose: Tribe member card for Aoife Murphy — the Wild Atlantic Way hiker who used her Trail 28L for six days.
prompts.emotional_role: Recognition — for outdoor-oriented women, this is their future self
prompts.commercial_role: Social proof for the Trail line via community storytelling
prompts.alt_text: Aoife Murphy, Adventure Hiker, on the Wild Atlantic Way with TrailVolt Trail 28L
prompts.short_prompt: Wide editorial-style landscape photo of a woman hiking coastal Irish cliffs with a Forest Green trail pack — Wild Atlantic Way, overcast sky, golden light break on horizon.
prompts.detailed_prompt: A wide editorial landscape photograph — the format is 2:1, panoramic. The Wild Atlantic Way in County Donegal: dramatic coastal cliffs dropping to the Atlantic, green headlands, overcast maritime sky with a single strip of golden-orange light at the sea horizon. A woman, late 20s, athletic build, wearing outdoor clothing (dark waterproof jacket, trail leggings), is visible in the mid-ground walking a coastal path that runs along the cliff edge. She is not posed — she is walking with purpose, looking ahead along the path. She is not looking at camera. The TrailVolt Trail 28L in Forest Green is on her back — full and taut, compression straps engaged. The Forest Green pack reads clearly against the dark rocks and green headland. The scale: person and pack together fill roughly 20-30% of the frame width — the landscape dominates, establishing the epic scale of her journey. The wide 2:1 crop creates a cinematic scope. The light: a blend of overcast ambient and a warm strip of light at the horizon catching the top of the cliffs. The feeling: this is a real day on the Wild Atlantic Way — earned, elemental, authentic.
prompts.negative_prompt: indoor or studio setting, generic mountain scenery (Swiss Alps), sunny blue-sky day, too much in-focus close-up of person (must be mid-ground), text on pack, logo on clothing, posed portrait framing, looking at camera, tropical or dry terrain
prompts.style_notes: The tribe cards are documentary-editorial — not brand photography. They must look like someone who happened to be on the trail that day took this photograph.
prompts.composition_notes: Wide 2:1 panoramic. Person in the middle-left third of the frame. Landscape dominates. The dramatic coastal environment is the story — the pack is clearly present but not front-and-centre.
prompts.lighting_notes: Overcast ambient from above, warm golden strip from sea horizon. Real Irish light — muted, dramatic, maritime.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: Aoife quote from site: 'Six days on the Wild Atlantic Way. I didn't miss a single thing I left behind.' This image is her six days in one frame.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Stubble & Co has no community photography. This is TrailVolt's strategic differentiation — real people in real places.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The inverted triangle points toward the horizon — the Wild Atlantic Way horizon. This is the brand's adventure direction made literal.

prompts.section: Tribe Cards — Marco Lazzarini, Digital Nomad, Barcelona
prompts.purpose: Tribe card for Marco — the digital nomad who has taken the Travel 30L to 14 countries.
prompts.emotional_role: Freedom — the life of the person who has made travel frictionless
prompts.commercial_role: Social proof for the Travel line via the digital nomad archetype
prompts.alt_text: Marco Lazzarini, Digital Nomad, at Barcelona airport with TrailVolt Travel 30L
prompts.short_prompt: Wide editorial photo of a man mid-stride in a Mediterranean airport terminal, Slate Grey travel pack on his back, gate signage in background, purposeful movement.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Wide editorial photograph in a modern Mediterranean airport terminal — Barcelona El Prat or similar: wide airy concourse, high ceilings with natural light, departure gate signs partially visible in background. A man, early 30s, Italian appearance, smart casual (dark blue shirt, chinos, leather trainers), mid-stride moving along the terminal floor. He is not looking at camera — his gaze is forward toward the gates. The TrailVolt Travel 30L in Slate Grey is on his back — clearly a carry-on, structured and appropriately sized. His movement is confident and purposeful — this is someone who does this every week. A small laptop sleeve or document sleeve partially visible in a side pocket. The wide 2:1 crop includes the terminal architecture — the human story is in context. Natural terminal ambient lighting. The Slate Grey pack is clearly distinguishable from black in the mixed lighting of the terminal.
prompts.negative_prompt: posed composition, camera-facing, generic airport stock photo style, wheeled luggage alongside, chaotic crowd behind, close-up portrait (must be wide shot showing environment), text on pack, logos on clothing, dark or dingy terminal
prompts.style_notes: This is documentary-editorial. The buyer should feel the life Marco leads — and want it.
prompts.composition_notes: Wide 2:1. Marco in mid-left frame, moving right. Terminal architecture fills the background. The pack is clearly present and correctly Slate Grey.
prompts.lighting_notes: Natural terminal ambient — mixed warm and cool sources.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: Marco: '14 countries. Never checked a bag.' This image is that confidence in one frame.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: The specific airport environment removes the genericness from the lifestyle claim.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: The Travel line's Slate Grey is described as 'most popular for Travel' — Marco confirms it.

prompts.section: Tribe Cards — Sarah Kim, Product Designer, Dublin
prompts.purpose: Tribe card for Sarah — the Dublin product designer who uses the Everyday 22L for gym-office-dinner.
prompts.emotional_role: Urban identity — the person who has figured out how to move through city life without compromise
prompts.commercial_role: Social proof for the Everyday line — the urban professional buyer
prompts.alt_text: Sarah Kim, Product Designer, cycling through Dublin with TrailVolt Everyday 22L
prompts.short_prompt: Wide editorial photo of a woman cycling through Dublin city centre, Core Black everyday pack on her back, morning light, Georgian architecture, purposeful urban movement.
prompts.detailed_prompt: Wide editorial photograph on Dublin's streets — Georgian red brick buildings lining the road, early morning light casting long shadows across the pavement. A woman, late 20s, Korean-Irish appearance, wearing smart casual (dark colours, not sportswear), is cycling through the city on a commuter bicycle. She is not looking at the camera — she is focused on the road ahead. The TrailVolt Everyday 22L in Core Black is on her back — visibly structured and well-fitted, not slouching. The cycling helmet (dark, minimal, premium-looking) is on her head. The Liffey or a Dublin canal is faintly visible in the background of the wide shot. The wide 2:1 crop shows the full Dublin context — the Georgian streetscape is the setting, Sarah and the pack are the subject within it. Early morning golden hour light.
prompts.negative_prompt: posed composition, camera-facing, generic city cycling stock photo, mountain bike or sports bike (commuter bicycle), unfamiliar non-Dublin architecture, text on pack, logo on clothing, sports gear colours
prompts.style_notes: The Dublin specificity is essential — Georgian brick, wide streets, canal or Liffey visible. This must look unmistakably Dublin, not Amsterdam or London.
prompts.composition_notes: Wide 2:1. Sarah cycling left-to-right through the lower half of the frame. Georgian architecture fills the upper half. Morning light from the right.
prompts.lighting_notes: Golden hour morning light, directional from the right, catching the pack and the cyclist.
prompts.why_this_image_fits_trailvolt: Sarah: 'Gym. Office. Dinner. One pack. I stopped thinking about what to carry.' This is that freedom in motion.
prompts.reference_site_lesson: Cycling in a specific city is more powerful than generic urban movement — the city IS part of the product story.
prompts.brand_folder_cue: Dublin is where TrailVolt was designed. Sarah's daily route is the same city that inspired the product.
